finding: A grammatical form (imperfective aspect) increased inferences of intentionality in a target, mediated by richer imaginative detail.
Memory for individual items was biased toward aggregate statistics of the set, indicating hierarchical integration rather than independent encoding.
A gustatory sensation modulated moral judgment intensity, with the effect moderated by a stable individual trait.
A bodily-purity prime shifted stated political attitudes and moral judgments, with effects persisting to the broader domain.
A mortality cue produced opposing responses across cultural groups: self-protective behavior in one group and other-protective behavior in another.
A brief incidental visual exposure shifted voting intentions and political attitudes, with effects persisting over months.
Emotional intensity modulated the chosen regulation strategy, with lower intensity favoring engagement-based approaches and higher intensity favoring disengagement.
A behavioral style (dominance) produced negative social evaluation in one demographic context but not another, indicating race as a moderator.
Local-group status predicted subjective well-being more strongly than broader socioeconomic status, mediated by feelings of power and acceptance.
Priming a concept of individual agency reduced support for redistributive policies and increased tolerance of inequality.
Sexualized female bodies showed a perceptual pattern typical of objects (absence of inversion effect), whereas sexualized male bodies showed a pattern typical of persons.
Reframing an issue in terms of a moral foundation held more strongly by one political group reduced the ideological gap in attitudes.
Participation in a domain-specific task (memory testing) increased self-perceived age in older adults but not younger ones, with the effect specific to that domain.
A perceived-value cue modulated a gender group's negative reaction to sexual imagery, with the modulation limited to that gender group.
A cue to social progress increased perceived discrimination against the dominant group, but only among those who saw the status hierarchy as legitimate.
Salience of demographic shifts increased political conservatism, mediated by perceived group-status threat.
Reduced belief in free will decreased support for retributive punishment while leaving consequentialist attitudes unaffected.
Hierarchical rank systematically altered acoustic properties of speech and was accurately inferred from these acoustic cues.
Weak negotiating alternatives created low anchors that suppressed first offers and worsened outcomes, despite producing feelings of greater power.
Priming a protection agent increased nonmoral risk-taking, mediated by perceived dangerousness and moderated by belief in that agent's protective reliability.
